title: Modification of Charge and Spin Textures by Light Chemical Substitution in Eu(Al$_{1-x}$Ga$_{x}$)$_4$ ($x=0.1$)
authors: Zétény Bacsó, Fellipe Carneiro, Kevin Allen, Aly H. Abdeldaim, Rebecca Scatena, Jaime M. Moya, Emilia Morosan, Alessandro Bombardi, Roger D. Johnson
abstract: We present the results of a resonant X-ray diffraction experiment, resolving both charge and spin textures in the intermetallic topological magnet Eu(Al$_{1-x}$Ga$_{x}$)$_4$, $x$ = 0.1. Below $\approx$ 75 K the system develops a charge density wave (CDW) with propagation vector kCDW ~ (0, 0, 0.18). The CDW order parameter grows monotonically on cooling until ~ 15 K, when a sudden decrease in the CDW amplitude occurs. Pairs of magnetic satellites of the (0, 0, 8) Bragg reflection corresponding to two distinct domains, k = ($\pm δ_\text{m}$, 0, 0), k2 = (0, $\pmδ_\text{m}$, 0), $δ_\text{m} = 0.2002(4)$ were studied at the Eu L3 edge, appearing below TN = 14.8 K. Our measurement of TN is exactly coincident with the sudden drop in the CDW amplitude, which suggests strong coupling between the charge and spin orders, as observed in other compounds of the Eu(Al$_{1-x}$Ga$_{x}$)$_4$ series. Azimuthal measurements revealed a single helical spin arrangement with an elliptical envelope of $μ_\text{Y}/μ_\text{Z}$ = 1.19(6) for the k2 domain, and a tilted helical (helicoidal) spin arrangement for the k1 domain, with $μ_\text{Y}/μ_\text{Z}$ = 1.14(4) and $μ_\text{X}/μ_\text{Z}$ = 0.20(2) that may be hidden for the k2 domain due to multiple subdomains. Temperature evolution of the magnetic satellite intensities in linear and circularly polarised light found the respective ratio to be invariant with temperature, suggesting a single magnetic phase below TN. This behaviour is unlike the x = 0 material, in which a spin density wave forms first, transitioning to a helical ground state on cooling through intermediate phases. Future theoretical work on the Eu electronic ground state, supported by related experiments, will help understand the effects of Ga substitution on the evolution of the magnetic structure.
summary: This experimental study uses resonant X-ray diffraction to characterize the coupled charge and spin ordering in Eu(Al1-xGa_x)4. The researchers found evidence for a Charge Density Wave and a complex magnetic structure below the Néel temperature. The work highlights the strong coupling between these two orders and details the specific helical nature of the magnetic moments in different crystal domains.
main_problem: The study investigates how light chemical substitution modifies the coupled charge and spin textures in the topological magnet Eu(Al1-xGa_x)4.
main_result: The system exhibits strong coupling between the Charge Density Wave (CDW) and magnetic order, with the magnetic structure characterized as a single helical arrangement in the k2 domain and a tilted helicoid in the k1 domain below the Néel temperature.
method: Resonant X-ray diffraction experiments were performed at the Eu L3 edge, utilizing polarization analysis and azimuthal measurements to resolve both charge and spin modulations.
model_or_system: The physical system is the intermetallic topological magnet Eu(Al1-xGa_x)4, where the substitution of Al by Ga ($x=0.1$) is used to tune the electronic and magnetic properties. The analysis focuses on the interplay between CDW formation and long-range magnetic ordering.
key_observables: CDW propagation vector (kCDW ~ (0, 0, 0.18)), magnetic satellite positions (k = (±δ_m, 0, 0) and (0, ±δ_m, 0)), and the ratio of intensities measured with linear vs. circularly polarized light.
important_parameters: Temperature (especially the transition temperatures $\approx 75 	ext{ K}$ for CDW and $T_N = 14.8 	ext{ K}$ for magnetism), and the substitution concentration ($x=0.1$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed magnetic scattering can be modeled using elliptical helix or helicoidal structures, and that the temperature invariance of the polarization ratio suggests a single magnetic phase below $T_N$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the temperature evolution of CDW intensity showing a sharp drop near 15 K, and show magnetic satellites in reciprocal space, with further analysis using azimuthal dependence to model the spin structure.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the presence of CDW and magnetic order, noting the coincidence of the CDW drop and $T_N$. It then uses azimuthal measurements to fit the magnetic satellite intensities, distinguishing between different types of helical spin arrangements in the two observed magnetic domains.
why_interesting: The strong coupling observed between charge and spin order in a topological magnet provides a rich platform for studying emergent quantum phenomena, which is relevant to understanding correlated electron physics in novel materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 3. The energy dependence of the (0, δm, 8) magnetic satellite intensity (blue), showing a large resonant enhance- ment at 6.9715 keV, just below the L3 edge of the known magnetic ion Eu2+ at 6.977 keV. For reference, the x-ray fluorescence measured away from the diffraction condition is shown (orange). Inset: Diffraction intensities of opposite po- larised circular light (dashed and solid lines, respectively). Both dataset were measured at Tbase, and normalized to the maximum resonant intensity at ψ = 250◦(see Fig. 7 for definiton of azimuthal angle ψ).

FIG. 1. Evolution of the (1, 1, 8.18) CDW diffraction in- tensity as function of temperature. Inset: An example the CDW intensity measured along k at Tbase = 7.5 K. The peak splitting was also observed for the (1, 1, 8) structural peak, suggesting it originates in structural inhomogeneities.

FIG. 2. The (0, 0, 8) structural diffraction intensity b) e) (green) and its four magnetic satellites a) c) (red) and d) f) (blue), measured in the h and k reciprocal space directions. The respective magnetic wave vector was measured to be δh = δk = 0.2002(4). Measurements were carried out at Tbase = 7.45 ± 0.08 K. The above colour code is adopted throughout the paper unless stated otherwise, to differentiate the two k- domains propagating along h and k, respectively. Note that the data were renormalized to the maximum intensity of the (0, 0, 8) peak measured along k.

FIG. 4. The temperature evolution of the (0, 0.2, 8) magnetic satellite measured on cooling. Long-range magnetic order de- velops at TN = 14.8 K. Inset: the normalized ratio of inten- sities measured with linear (Iσ) and circularly polarised (IC) incident light.

FIG. 5. The azimuthal dependence of the a) k1 = (-0.2, 0, 8) and b) k2 = (0, 0.2, 8) magnetic satellite intensities. Elliptical helix model fit is shown as solid red and blue lines. The helicoidal model fit is shown by the dashed line on panel a). The zero point in azimuth corresponds to a sample orientation for which the (1, 0, 0) reciprocal space vector points away from the source, and within the scattering plane.
